revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Osuwa Daiko]][[en:Osuwa Daiko]][[ja:御諏訪太鼓]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Osuwa Daiko]][[en:Osuwa Daiko]][[ja:御諏訪太鼓]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Osuwa Daiko]][[en:Osuwa Daiko]][[ja:御諏訪太鼓]]
{{Use dmy dates|date=August 2022}} {{Infobox musical artist | name = Osuwa Daiko | image = OsuwaDaikoLogo.png | caption = Logo for Osuwa Daiko | landscape = yes | origin = {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|en|Okaya, Nagano|WD=Q838672|lt=Okaya, Nagano Prefecture}}, Japan | years_active = {{start date|1951}}-present | genre = {{ill|Taiko|en|Taiko|WD=Q221769|lt=Taiko}} | label = | website = {{url|http://www.osuwadaiko.com/}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Osuwa Daiko'''|御諏訪太鼓}} is a Japanese percussion group credited as the earliest groups to develop and perform the ensemble-style of {{ill|Taiko|en|Taiko|WD=Q221769|lt=Taiko}} called ''kumi-daiko''. Formed in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|en|Okaya, Nagano|WD=Q838672|lt=Okaya}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} in 1951 and founded by [[Daihachi Oguchi]], Osuwa Daiko created a style of performance independent from performance during festivals, theatrical performance, and religious ceremonies, and transformed them into an ensemble performance. Contemporary taiko performance in Japan and in the rest of the world is widely based on the practices of Osuwa Daiko.
==History== {{see also|Daihachi Oguchi}} Osuwa Daiko was founded by Daihachi Oguchi in 1951 in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}},<ref name=Kagayama>{{cite news|last=Kageyama|first=Yuri|title=Master Japanese drummer Oguchi dies|url=http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/news/world/2008-06-26-1510076401_x.htm|access-date=6 September 2013|newspaper=[[USA Today]]|date=27 June 2008}}</ref> who was inspired by his background as a jazz drummer and was interested in expanding the scope of percussion traditions in Japan.<ref name=Kobayashi>{{cite web|last=Kobayashi|first=Kim Noriko|title=Asian Women Kick Ass: A Study of Gender Issues within Canadian Kumi-Daiko|url=https://www.canfolkmusic.ca/index.php/cfmb/article/viewFile/102/101|work=Musique Folklorique Canadien|publisher=University of British Columbia|access-date=7 August 2022}}</ref> Although {{ill|Taiko|en|Taiko|WD=Q221769|lt=Taiko}} drums had existed for several centuries beforehand, Osuwa Daiko was the first to use several, differently-sized drums in an ensemble format.<ref name=Powell>{{cite book|last=Powell|first=Kimberly|title=Thinking Comprehensively About Education: Spaces of Educative Possibility and their Implications for Public Policy|year=2012|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1136318474|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlZh8b-YXMgC&pg=PT259|editor=Ezekiel Dixon-Román & Edmund W. Gordon}}</ref> This was in contrast to taiko performances that often accompanied theatrical performances such as [[gagaku]] or Buddhist ceremonies like {{ill|Obon|en|Obon|WD=Q1154888|lt=Obon}}, though these styles influenced Osuwa Daiko's performance work.<ref name=Powell2>{{cite journal|last=Powell|first=Kimberly|title=Composing Sound Identity in Taiko Drumming|journal=Anthropology & Education Quarterly|year=2012|volume=43|issue=1|page=104|doi=10.1111/j.1548-1492.2011.01159.x}}</ref>
Many of the initial performers in Osuwa Daiko were not professional musicians, and so parts were partitioned out across different drums into simpler rhythms for individuals to play. For instance, the ''{{ill|Shime-daiko|en|Shime-daiko|WD=Q4148082|lt=Shime-daiko}}'', a high-pitched drum, carried the role of keeping the {{ill|Tempo|en|Tempo|WD=Q189214|lt=Tempo}}. [[Taiko#Categorization|Several styles of medium-sized drums]] were used to play the "melody" of the piece and also incorporated [[:en:phrasing (music)|phrasing]]. A large ''[[:en:o-daiko|o-daiko]]'', using a simple rhythm, established a pulse for the rest of performers. Each performer was also equipped with several different drums at once, similar to a jazz drum set.<ref name=Panalaks>{{cite web|last=Panalaks|first=Miyako Saito|title=The Ma of Taiko|url=http://www.collectionscanada.gc.ca/obj/s4/f2/dsk3/ftp05/MQ63548.pdf|work=Dalhousie University|access-date=6 September 2013}}</ref>
One main goals of the group was to transform traditional taiko pieces performed at local festivals and temples into an ensemble format.<ref name=Bender>{{cite book|last=Bender|first=Shawn Morgan|title=Taiko Boom: Japanese Drumming in Place and Motion|year=2012|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0520272415|page=60|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-ne8y-7kzYYC}}</ref> After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, the Japanese cultural identity had been considerably weakened as a result of their defeat as well as through the incorporation of Western cultural values. However, Oguchi's reformulated pieces, based on pieces traditionally reserved for festivals and religious ceremonies, were a way to recultivate and redefine Japanese culture and identity.<ref name=Cecconi>{{cite news|last=Cecconi|first=Bruno|title=El taiko es totalmente contemporáneo|url=http://www.lanacion.com.ar/1123963-el-taiko-es-totalmente-contemporaneo|work=La Nacion|date=3 May 2009|publisher=SA LA NACION|access-date=6 September 2013|language=Spanish}}</ref>
Initially, performances by the group were restricted to regional venues such as inns and sometimes hotels in Japan.{{sfn|Bender|2012|p=60}} Osuwa Daiko started to become more visible later in the 1950s. Televised performances of the group were shown on {{ill|NHK|en|NHK|WD=Q212128|lt=NHK}}, one of the major television networks in Japan.{{sfn|Bender|2012|p=52}} Osuwa Daiko, and taiko performance in general, also gained international attention when they were featured at the 1964 Winter Olympics.<ref name=Varian>{{cite book|last=Varian|first=Heidi|title=The Way of Taiko: 2nd Edition|year=2013|publisher=Stone Bridge Press|isbn=978-1611720129|pages=28–29|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PuRSmQEACAAJ}}</ref> This televised performance also popularized taiko performance locally in Japan.{{sfn|Bender|2012|p=7}}
==Impact== In the development of taiko performance in an ensemble format, Osuwa Daiko has been considered one of the dominant, leading groups in Japan.<ref name=Mansfield>{{cite news|last=Mansfield|first=Stephen|title=Playing to the beat of the gods|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/culture/2013/03/17/books/playing-to-the-beat-of-the-gods/#.Uiom2_lesac|access-date=6 September 2013|newspaper={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|WD=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times}}|date=17 March 2013}}</ref> Specifically, their style of instrumentation, presentation on stage, {{ill|Kata|en|Kata|WD=Q40795|lt=Kata}}, and repertoire of pieces have largely contributed to how contemporary taiko is understood and practiced.{{sfn|Bender|2012|p=21}} In addition, Osuwa Daiko is known for its activity in training and teaching of other taiko groups in terms of technique and repertoire, particularly newer groups.{{sfn|Bender|2012|p=114}}
==References== {{reflist|2}}
{{Suwa Faith}}

Gordon}}</ref> This was in contrast to taiko performances that often accompanied theatrical performances such as [[gagaku]] or Buddhist ceremonies like {{ill|Obon|en|Obon|WD=Q1154888|lt=Obon|1=Obon}}, though these styles influenced Osuwa Daiko's performance work.<ref name=Powell2>{{cite journal|last=Powell|first=Kimberly|title=Composing Sound Identity in Taiko Drumming|journal=Anthropology & Education Quarterly|year=2012|volume=43|issue=1|page=104|doi=10.1111/j.1548-1492.2011.01159.x}}</ref>

[[Taiko#Categorization|Several styles of medium-sized drums]] were used to play the "melody" of the piece and also incorporated [[:en:phrasing (music)|phrasing]]. {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2022}}
{{Infobox musical artist
| name         = Osuwa Daiko
| image        = OsuwaDaikoLogo.png
| caption      = Logo for Osuwa Daiko
| landscape    = yes
| origin       = {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|en|Okaya, Nagano|WD=Q838672|lt=Okaya, Nagano Prefecture|1=Okaya, Nagano}}, Japan
| years_active = {{start date|1951}}-present
| genre        = {{ill|Taiko|en|Taiko|WD=Q221769|lt=Taiko|1=Taiko}}
| label        =
| website      = {{url|http://www.osuwadaiko.com/}}
}}

{{nihongo|'''Osuwa Daiko'''|御諏訪太鼓}} is a Japanese percussion group credited as the earliest groups to develop and perform the ensemble-style of {{ill|Taiko|en|Taiko|WD=Q221769|lt=Taiko|1=Taiko}} called ''kumi-daiko''.  Formed in {{ill|Okaya, Nagano|en|Okaya, Nagano|WD=Q838672|lt=Okaya|1=Okaya, Nagano}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} in 1951 and founded by [[Daihachi Oguchi]], Osuwa Daiko created a style of performance independent from performance during festivals, theatrical performance, and religious ceremonies, and transformed them into an ensemble performance.  Contemporary taiko performance in Japan and in the rest of the world is widely based on the practices of Osuwa Daiko.

==History==
{{see also|Daihachi Oguchi}}
Osuwa Daiko was founded by Daihachi Oguchi in 1951 in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}},<ref name=Kagayama>{{cite news|last=Kageyama|first=Yuri|title=Master Japanese drummer Oguchi dies|url=http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/news/world/2008-06-26-1510076401_x.htm|access-date=6 September 2013|newspaper=[[USA Today]]|date=27 June 2008}}</ref>  who was inspired by his background as a jazz drummer and was interested in expanding the scope of percussion traditions in Japan.<ref name=Kobayashi>{{cite web|last=Kobayashi|first=Kim Noriko|title=Asian Women Kick Ass: A Study of Gender Issues within Canadian Kumi-Daiko|url=https://www.canfolkmusic.ca/index.php/cfmb/article/viewFile/102/101|work=Musique Folklorique Canadien|publisher=University of British Columbia|access-date=7 August 2022}}</ref>  Although {{ill|Taiko|en|Taiko|WD=Q221769|lt=Taiko|1=Taiko}} drums had existed for several centuries beforehand, Osuwa Daiko was the first to use several, differently-sized drums in an ensemble format.<ref name=Powell>{{cite book|last=Powell|first=Kimberly|title=Thinking Comprehensively About Education: Spaces of Educative Possibility and their Implications for Public Policy|year=2012|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1136318474|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xlZh8b-YXMgC&pg=PT259|editor=Ezekiel Dixon-Román & Edmund W. Gordon}}</ref>  This was in contrast to taiko performances that often accompanied theatrical performances such as [[gagaku]] or Buddhist ceremonies like {{ill|Obon|en|Obon|WD=Q1154888|lt=Obon|1=Obon}}, though these styles influenced Osuwa Daiko's performance work.<ref name=Powell2>{{cite journal|last=Powell|first=Kimberly|title=Composing Sound Identity in Taiko Drumming|journal=Anthropology & Education Quarterly|year=2012|volume=43|issue=1|page=104|doi=10.1111/j.1548-1492.2011.01159.x}}</ref>

Many of the initial performers in Osuwa Daiko were not professional musicians, and so parts were partitioned out across different drums into simpler rhythms for individuals to play.  For instance, the ''{{ill|Shime-daiko|en|Shime-daiko|WD=Q4148082|lt=Shime-daiko|1=Shime-daiko}}'', a high-pitched drum, carried the role of keeping the {{ill|Tempo|en|Tempo|WD=Q189214|lt=Tempo|1=Tempo}}.  [[Taiko#Categorization|Several styles of medium-sized drums]] were used to play the "melody" of the piece and also incorporated [[:en:phrasing (music)|phrasing]].  A large ''[[:en:o-daiko|o-daiko]]'', using a simple rhythm, established a pulse for the rest of performers.  Each performer was also equipped with several different drums at once, similar to a jazz drum set.<ref name=Panalaks>{{cite web|last=Panalaks|first=Miyako Saito|title=The Ma of Taiko|url=http://www.collectionscanada.gc.ca/obj/s4/f2/dsk3/ftp05/MQ63548.pdf|work=Dalhousie University|access-date=6 September 2013}}</ref>

One main goals of the group was to transform traditional taiko pieces performed at local festivals and temples into an ensemble format.<ref name=Bender>{{cite book|last=Bender|first=Shawn Morgan|title=Taiko Boom: Japanese Drumming in Place and Motion|year=2012|publisher=University of California Press|isbn=978-0520272415|page=60|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-ne8y-7kzYYC}}</ref>  After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, the Japanese cultural identity had been considerably weakened as a result of their defeat as well as through the incorporation of Western cultural values.  However, Oguchi's reformulated pieces, based on pieces traditionally reserved for festivals and religious ceremonies, were a way to recultivate and redefine Japanese culture and identity.<ref name=Cecconi>{{cite news|last=Cecconi|first=Bruno|title=El taiko es totalmente contemporáneo|url=http://www.lanacion.com.ar/1123963-el-taiko-es-totalmente-contemporaneo|work=La Nacion|date=3 May 2009|publisher=SA LA NACION|access-date=6 September 2013|language=Spanish}}</ref>

Initially, performances by the group were restricted to regional venues such as inns and sometimes hotels in Japan.{{sfn|Bender|2012|p=60}}  Osuwa Daiko started to become more visible later in the 1950s.  Televised performances of the group were shown on {{ill|NHK|en|NHK|WD=Q212128|lt=NHK|1=NHK}}, one of the major television networks in Japan.{{sfn|Bender|2012|p=52}}  Osuwa Daiko, and taiko performance in general, also gained international attention when they were featured  at the 1964 Winter Olympics.<ref name=Varian>{{cite book|last=Varian|first=Heidi|title=The Way of Taiko: 2nd Edition|year=2013|publisher=Stone Bridge Press|isbn=978-1611720129|pages=28–29|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PuRSmQEACAAJ}}</ref>  This televised performance also popularized taiko performance locally in Japan.{{sfn|Bender|2012|p=7}}

==Impact==
In the development of taiko performance in an ensemble format, Osuwa Daiko has been considered one of the dominant, leading groups in Japan.<ref name=Mansfield>{{cite news|last=Mansfield|first=Stephen|title=Playing to the beat of the gods|url=http://www.japantimes.co.jp/culture/2013/03/17/books/playing-to-the-beat-of-the-gods/#.Uiom2_lesac|access-date=6 September 2013|newspaper={{ill|The Japan Times|en|The Japan Times|WD=Q1190886|lt=The Japan Times|1=The Japan Times}}|date=17 March 2013}}</ref>  Specifically, their style of instrumentation, presentation on stage, {{ill|Kata|en|Kata|WD=Q40795|lt=Kata|1=Kata}}, and repertoire of pieces have largely contributed to how contemporary taiko is understood and practiced.{{sfn|Bender|2012|p=21}}  In addition, Osuwa Daiko is known for its activity in training and teaching of other taiko groups in terms of technique and repertoire, particularly newer groups.{{sfn|Bender|2012|p=114}}

==References==
{{reflist|2}}

{{Suwa Faith}}
